subject: Mathematics

unit.unitTitle: Statistics: Tally Charts and Frequency Tables
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit you will become a real data detective. Statistics is all about asking good questions, collecting information to answer them, and then organising that information so it tells a clear story. Every day the people around you gather data without even naming it. When Amina asks her class which drink they would like for the Eid party, when Yusuf counts how many goats come home each evening, or when Halima records how many customers visit the family stall at the Hargeisa market, they are all doing statistics.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 3 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Conduct simple investigations to answer a statistical question.
Record and organise data using a tally chart, grouping marks in fives.
Convert a tally chart into a frequency table.
Read and make a simple pictogram where one picture stands for one item.
Interpret data to find the most and least common results.
Work out totals and simple fractions from a set of data.
Make and test a conjecture using data you collect yourself.
Describe your data clearly using the right mathematical words.

concepts.title: What Is Statistics and Why We Collect Data
concepts.explanation: How do you find things out? Very often, you ask a question and then find a way to gather information to answer it. That is exactly what statistics is. It begins with a question you truly want answered, such as “Which drink should we order for the class party?” or “Which sport is the most popular in our class?” The information you gather is called data. Data can be all sorts of things: the drinks your friends like, the number of goats in each family's herd, the colours of cars passing the masjid, or the weather on…
concepts.example: Let us follow a real example. Imagine you are planning a party for your class and you want to know what drinks to order. You could ask everyone, “What is your favourite drink?” and write down each answer. But if you…

concepts.title: Tally Charts and Tally Marks
concepts.explanation: A tally chart is a way to collect data quickly while it is happening. It uses small marks called tally marks, and the clever part is that the marks are grouped in fives. Grouping in fives makes the marks much faster to count at the end, because you can count in fives instead of one at a time. Here is exactly how tally marks work:
concepts.example: For 1 to 4, you draw single upright lines: | , || , ||| , ||||

concepts.title: Frequency Tables
concepts.explanation: Once your tally chart is complete, you can turn it into a frequency table. The word frequency simply means *how many times something happens*. A frequency table shows this count as a number instead of tally marks. This makes it very easy to read, because you no longer need to count the marks each time -- the number is right there in front of you. To build a frequency table from a tally chart, you go row by row, count the tally marks, and write the total as a number. Here is the frequency table for our drinks…
concepts.example: Notice how the frequency table holds exactly the same information as the tally chart, just in a tidier form. Seven learners chose water, nine chose milk, and four chose juice. A frequency table is like a clean copy you…

concepts.title: Reading a Pictogram
concepts.explanation: There is one more friendly way to show data: the pictogram. A pictogram uses small pictures to stand for the things you counted. In a simple pictogram, one picture means one item. If you are showing how many goats each family owns, you might draw one small goat picture for each goat, all lined up in neat rows so they are easy to compare. Suppose Idris makes a pictogram of the fruit sold at the family stall one morning. He draws one mango picture for each mango sold and one banana picture for each banana sold. If…
concepts.example: Pictograms are lovely because you can *see* the answer without reading any numbers at all -- the tallest or longest row simply looks the biggest. To read a pictogram, you count the pictures in each row, just as you…

concepts.title: Interpreting Data
concepts.explanation: Collecting and organising data is only half the job. The exciting part is interpreting it -- looking at the numbers and working out what they mean. When you interpret data you look beyond the numbers and answer real questions about them. Here are the questions a good data detective always asks: Which category is the most common (has the highest frequency)?
concepts.example: Which category is the least common (has the lowest frequency)?

concepts.title: Making and Testing Conjectures
concepts.explanation: A conjecture is an idea about what you think will happen before you collect any data. It is a thoughtful guess. In statistics we love conjectures, because we can test them by collecting real data and then checking whether the data agrees. Here is a famous example. You might think, “If I flip a coin 20 times, I will get more heads than tails.” That is a conjecture. To test it, you actually flip a coin 20 times and record each result with a tally mark under “Heads” or “Tails.” At the end you count the two rows and…
concepts.example: Another example uses a dice. Omar throws a dice 12 times and conjectures, “The dice will land on each number twice.” He records his throws with tally marks, then counts. Almost always the dice does not land on each…

methods.title: How to make a tally chart
methods.example: How many marks are shown by two fives and three single marks?
steps: Write a label for each category down the side.
Each time you see an answer, add one tally mark in the correct row.
Group the marks in fives, drawing the fifth across the first four.
When you finish, count each row to find its total.

methods.title: How to turn a tally chart into a frequency table
methods.example: Show the number 7 as a tally.
steps: Look at the first row of tally marks.
Count the fives first, then add the leftover single marks.
Write that total as a number in the frequency column.
Repeat for every row.

methods.title: How to interpret a frequency table
methods.example: A fruit survey has these tallies: Apples one five and two more, Bananas two fives and two more, Oranges one five. Write the frequency table.
steps: Find the highest number for the most common category.
Find the lowest number for the least common category.
Add every frequency to find the total.
For a fraction, put the count on top and the total on the bottom.

methods.title: Find the total surveyed
methods.example: A tally chart shows 9 learners like apples and 7 like bananas. How many learners were surveyed in total?
steps: Solution: Add the two frequencies: 9 + 7 = 16 learners.
To find how many took part altogether, you always add up every category.
Check the find the total surveyed result carefully.

methods.title: Most and least popular
methods.example: A sports frequency table shows Football 12, Swimming 8, Tennis 5, Basketball 7. Which sport is most popular and which is least popular?
steps: Solution: The most popular is the one with the highest frequency: Football with 12.
The least popular is the one with the lowest frequency: Tennis with 5.
Reading a frequency table means comparing the numbers.

methods.title: Add two categories
methods.example: Using the same sports table, how many learners chose swimming or basketball?
steps: Solution: “Or” means add the two frequencies together: 8 + 7 = 15 learners chose swimming or basketball.
Check the add two categories result carefully.
Check the add two categories result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Count the tally marks
workedExamples.prompt: How many marks are shown by two fives and three single marks?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count the fives first: 5 and 5 make 10. Then add the three loose marks: 10 + 3 = 13. Counting the fives before the singles is the fast, safe way.

workedExamples.title: Draw the right tally
workedExamples.prompt: Show the number 7 as a tally.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Seven is one group of five and two more single marks: ||||̸ ||. That is 5 + 2 = 7. Remember that the fifth mark goes across the first four to make the group of five.

workedExamples.title: Tally chart to frequency table
workedExamples.prompt: A fruit survey has these tallies: Apples one five and two more, Bananas two fives and two more, Oranges one five. Write the frequency table.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Apples = 5 + 2 = 7. Bananas = 5 + 5 + 2 = 12. Oranges = 5. So the frequency table reads Apples 7, Bananas 12, Oranges 5. We simply counted each row and wrote the total as a number.

workedExamples.title: Find the total surveyed
workedExamples.prompt: A tally chart shows 9 learners like apples and 7 like bananas. How many learners were surveyed in total?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the two frequencies: 9 + 7 = 16 learners. To find how many took part altogether, you always add up every category.

workedExamples.title: Most and least popular
workedExamples.prompt: A sports frequency table shows Football 12, Swimming 8, Tennis 5, Basketball 7. Which sport is most popular and which is least popular?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The most popular is the one with the highest frequency: Football with 12. The least popular is the one with the lowest frequency: Tennis with 5. Reading a frequency table means comparing the numbers.

workedExamples.title: Add two categories
workedExamples.prompt: Using the same sports table, how many learners chose swimming or basketball?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: “Or” means add the two frequencies together: 8 + 7 = 15 learners chose swimming or basketball.

workedExamples.title: A fraction from data
workedExamples.prompt: In a survey of 28 learners, 5 chose strawberry ice cream. What fraction chose strawberry?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The number who chose strawberry goes on top and the total goes on the bottom: 5/28. So 5 out of every 28 learners chose strawberry.

workedExamples.title: Test a conjecture
workedExamples.prompt: Amina flips a coin 20 times and conjectures there will be more heads than tails. Her tally shows Heads 9 and Tails 11. Was her conjecture right?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Compare the counts: Heads 9, Tails 11. There were more tails than heads, so her conjecture was wrong this time. Because coin flips are chance, a different try might give a different result -- and that is a perfectly good thing to discover.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Which sport was the most popular, and which was the least popular?

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: How many learners chose swimming or tennis?
workedExamples.solution: Summer 9, Winter 7, Spring 2, Autumn 2; Summer was most popular.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: How many learners took part in the survey altogether?

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: How many more learners chose football than basketball?
workedExamples.solution: Use the section 2: core practice guidance and explain each step.

activities.title: Coin Flip Investigation
activities.materials: a coin, paper, and a pencil.
steps: Make a conjecture: “If I flip a coin 20 times, there will be more heads than tails.” Write it down.
Make a tally chart with two rows: “Heads” and “Tails.”
Flip the coin 20 times and record each result with a tally mark.
Count the total for each row. Write two things you found out from your data.
Repeat the experiment. Did you get the same results? Was your conjecture right both times?

activities.title: Family Survey Project
steps: Think of a question to ask, such as “What is your favourite fruit?” or “What is your favourite prayer time to attend the masjid?”
Make a tally chart with a row for each possible answer.
Ask at least 10 people in your family or neighbourhood and record their answers with tally marks.
Convert your tally chart into a frequency table.
Write three things your data tells you, including the most and least popular answer.

activities.title: Ten-Minute Traffic Count
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a safe place to watch a road or path.
steps: Make a tally chart with rows for the things you will count, such as cars, buses, bicycles, and people walking.
Watch for exactly ten minutes and add one tally mark for each thing that passes.
Count the fives first, then the leftover marks, to find each total.
Which was the most common? Which was the least common? How many passed altogether?
Tally charts are perfect for this because things pass quickly and one mark is fast to draw.

activities.title: Make a Pictogram of Your Home
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and some colouring pencils.
steps: Choose something to count around your home, such as cups, spoons, or books.
Count how many there are in two or three different rooms.
Draw a pictogram: one small picture for each object, lined up neatly in a row for each room.
Which room had the most? You can see the answer just by looking at the longest row.

activities.title: Cereal Box Investigation (Think Like a Mathematician)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and six differently coloured markers or crayons.
steps: Imagine a company gives one of six free gifts inside every packet, and each packet has a random gift.
Make a conjecture: how many packets do you think you need to buy to collect all six gifts?
Use the coloured markers to stand for the gifts. Close your eyes and pick one to be your “gift” each time.
Keep a tally of which gift you get, and count how many “packets” you needed to collect all six.
Repeat five times. What was the average number of packets needed? Was your conjecture close?

activities.title: Weather Watch (Week-Long Investigation)
steps: Make a tally chart with rows for Sunny, Cloudy, Rainy and Windy.
Each day for one week, add one tally mark for that day's weather.
At the end of the week, turn your tally chart into a frequency table.
Which weather was most common? Make a conjecture about next week and test it!

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand tally charts and frequency tables best? What was the trickiest part? Which of your conjectures turned out to be right, and which were wrong? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Tally Mark Rule
rules.text: Marks are grouped in fives. The fifth mark goes across the first four. To count, say the fives first (5, 10, 15) then add the leftover single marks.

rules.title: Frequency Rule
rules.text: Frequency means how many times something happens. A frequency table shows the same data as a tally chart, but as numbers.

rules.title: Interpreting Data Rule
rules.text: Most common = the highest frequency. Least common = the lowest frequency. Total = add every frequency. Fraction = the count on top, the total on the bottom.

terms: Asking many people the same question
Recording data with marks grouped in fives
A table showing counts as numbers
A chart that uses pictures to show data
An idea about what you think will happen
commonMistakes: Count the fives first, then the leftovers
Four marks then a separate fifth
Draw the fifth mark across the first four
Count on top, total on the bottom: 5/28

realProblems.prompt: A shopkeeper in Kismaayo records customers each hour: 12, 15, 23, 30, 28, 18, 14, 20. What was the total number of customers for the day, and which hour was busiest?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A class of 30 recorded the weather for a month: Sunny 12, Cloudy 8, Rainy 5, Windy 5. What fraction of days were rainy, and how many days were not sunny?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Fifteen football matches had these goals: 0 goals in 2 matches, 1 goal in 5, 2 goals in 4, 3 goals in 3, 4 goals in 1. What was the total number of goals scored in all the matches?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Twenty-four learners said how they travel to school: Walk 10, Car 8, Bus 4, Bicycle 2. Draw a tally chart to show this, and say which way was least common.
realProblems.answer: Walk ||||̸ ||||̸, Car ||||̸ |||, Bus ||||, Bicycle ||; least common was Bicycle.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Walk ||||̸ ||||̸, Car ||||̸ |||, Bus ||||, Bicycle ||; least common was Bicycle.

realProblems.prompt: During Eid, Aisha counts the sweets each of 5 children collects: 6, 4, 7, 5, 8. How many sweets in total, and what fraction did the child with 8 collect?
realProblems.answer: Total = 6 + 4 + 7 + 5 + 8 = 30 sweets; the child with 8 collected 8/30.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Total = 6 + 4 + 7 + 5 + 8 = 30 sweets; the child with 8 collected 8/30.

Recording data with marks grouped in fives

selfAssessment: I can conduct simple investigations to answer a statistical question.
I can record and organise data using a tally chart, grouping marks in fives.
I can convert a tally chart into a frequency table.
I can read and make a simple pictogram where one picture stands for one item.
I can interpret data to find the most and least common results.
I can work out totals and simple fractions from a set of data.
I can make and test a conjecture using data you collect yourself.
I can describe your data clearly using the right mathematical words.
